'''Luke Gale''' (born 22 June 1988) is a professional [[rugby league]] coach who is an assistant coach at [[Hull Kingston Rovers]] in the [[Super League]] and a former [[England national rugby league team|England]] international professional [[rugby league]] footballer who played as {{rlp|sh}} or {{rlp|so}}.

Having started his career at [[Leeds Rhinos]], he previously played for [[Doncaster R.L.F.C.|Doncaster]] on loan in the [[RFL Championship|National Leagues]], and [[London Broncos|Harlequins RL]], [[Bradford Bulls]], [[Castleford Tigers]] and [[Hull F.C.]]  in the [[Super League]] and for [[Keighley Cougars]] in the [[Championship (rugby league)|Championship]].<ref name="Statistics at rugbyleagueproject.org"/> He played occasionally as a {{rlp|hk}} earlier in his career.

==Biography==
Gale was born in [[Leeds, Yorkshire]], England on 22 June 1988.<ref name="castlefordtigers"/>

On 18 November 2025 it was announced that Gale had joined the coaching staff at [[Hull Kingston Rovers]] as an Assistant Coach on a two year deal

==Playing career==
===Doncaster RLFC===
A [[Leeds Rhinos|Leeds]] academy graduate, Gale was sent out on loan to [[Doncaster R.L.F.C.|Doncaster]] in 2007 and established himself as a first-choice player, winning the [[League 1 (rugby league)|National League Two]] Young Player of the Year award in 2008, in which he became the club's record points scorer in a season.{{citation needed|date=March 2023}}

===Harlequins RL===
Gale's form at Doncaster attracted interest from [[Super League]] clubs and he moved to London's [[London Broncos|Harlequins Rugby League]] team.{{citation needed|date=March 2023}}

===Bradford Bulls===
[[File:Luke Gale.JPG|right|thumb|Gale in action for Harlequins in 2010.]] 
Gale spent three years at Harlequins club before signing a two-year contract with [[Bradford Bulls]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Bradford Bulls sign Harlequins scrum-half Luke Gale |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/14246164 |website=BBC Sport |access-date=13 September 2023 |date=22 July 2011}}</ref>

Gale immediately became first-choice scrum-half for Bradford, scoring 143 points for the club in his début season. He signed a three-year extension to his contract at the start of 2013,<ref>{{cite web |title=Super League: Luke Gale signs new deal with Bradford Bulls |url=https://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/news/12196/8162358/super-league-luke-gale-signs-new-deal-with-bradford-bulls |website=Sky Sports |access-date=16 September 2023 |date=13 October 2012}}</ref> however he missed the first three months of the season due to injury and only made 16 appearances in total during the year. 
[[File:Luke Gale Bradford.jpg|right|thumb|Gale playing for Bradford in 2014]] 
Gale missed only one match for Bradford in 2014, however the team struggled on the field and the club was relegated from Super League at the end of the season, following which Gale left the club.

===Castleford Tigers===
Gale joined Castleford in 2015 as a replacement for the departing [[Marc Sneyd]], and scored a try on his début for the club against [[Wakefield Trinity]]. Gale enjoyed a productive season, scoring 205 points and becoming an integral part of the team that narrowly missed out on the Super League semi-finals. His form was recognised as he won the [[Albert Goldthorpe Medal]] at the end of the season.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Bower|first=Aaron|date=14 September 2015|title=Luke Gale wins Albert Goldthorpe Medal|url=https://www.totalrl.com/luke-gale-wins-albert-goldthorpe-medal/|access-date=2022-01-13|website=TotalRL}}</ref>

Gale was made acting captain for the [[2016 Castleford Tigers season|2016 season]] due to injury to [[Michael Shenton]].<ref>{{Cite web|last=Charles|first=Andy|date=11 February 2016|title=Luke Gale to deputise for injured Castleford captain Michael Shenton|url=https://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/news/13995/10162338/luke-gale-to-deputise-for-injured-castleford-captain-michael-shenton|access-date=2022-01-13|website=Sky Sports}}</ref> Another strong season in which he topped the league for try assists saw him retain his Albert Goldthorpe medal.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Shaw|first=Matthew|date=5 September 2016|title=Luke Gale wins the Albert Goldthorpe Medal again|url=https://www.totalrl.com/luke-gale-wins-albert-goldthorpe-medal-2/|access-date=2022-01-13|website=TotalRL}}</ref>

In 2017, Gale played an integral role in guiding Castleford to the [[League Leaders' Shield]], their first piece of major silverware since 1994, as well as leading the team to their maiden [[2017 Super League Grand Final|Grand Final]] appearance, which was lost 24–6 to [[Leeds Rhinos]].<ref>{{cite web|date=7 October 2017|title=Grand Final 2017: Castleford 6–24 Leeds Rhinos|url=https://www.bbc.com/sport/rugby-league/41536394|access-date=6 November 2019|work=BBC Sport}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|date=7 October 2017|title=Danny McGuire guides Leeds to Grand Final success over Castleford|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2017/oct/07/castleford-leeds-super-league-grand-final-match-report|access-date=6 November 2019|work=Guardian}}</ref> Undoubtedly Gale's best performance of the season was in Castleford's 23–22 victory over [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]] in their [[Super League XXII#Playoffs|Betfred Super League semi-final]], in which he converted a last-minute penalty to bring the game to golden point extra-time, before scoring the drop-goal which sent Castleford to [[Old Trafford]] for the first time in the club's history.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Cartwright|first=Phil|date=28 September 2017|title=Super League semi-final: Castleford Tigers 23–22 St Helens|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/41402940|access-date=2022-01-13|website=BBC Sport}}</ref> Just two weeks beforehand, he had undergone an emergency appendectomy operation.<ref>{{Cite web|date=3 October 2017|title=Luke Gale: Castleford scrum-half amazed by Grand Final opportunity after surgery|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/41483794|access-date=2022-01-13|website=BBC Sport}}</ref> The following week, Gale was named 2017 [[Man of Steel Awards|Steve Prescott MBE Man of Steel]] for his performances throughout the season, beating team-mate [[Zak Hardaker]] and [[Hull F.C.|Hull FC]]’s [[Albert Kelly]] to the award.<ref>{{Cite web|date=3 October 2017|title=Luke Gale: Castleford half-back wins 2017 Steve Prescott Man of Steel award|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/41488323|access-date=2022-01-13|website=BBC Sport}}</ref> He also won the Albert Goldthorpe Medal for a record-breaking third consecutive year.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Davis|first=Alex|date=11 September 2017|title=Luke Gale wins Albert Goldthorpe Medal for a third time|url=https://www.totalrl.com/luke-gale-wins-albert-goldthorpe-medal-third-time/|access-date=2022-01-13|website=TotalRL}}</ref>

In May 2018, Gale suffered a knee injury which kept him sidelined for three months.<ref>{{cite web |title=Luke Gale: Castleford Tigers half-back out for three months with fractured kneecap |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/43982262 |website=BBC Sport |access-date=13 September 2023 |date=2 May 2018}}</ref> He then missed the entire 2019 season after rupturing his achilles tendon in training.<ref>{{cite web |title=Luke Gale: Castleford Tigers & England half-back suffers further injury setback |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/46775640 |website=BBC Sport |access-date=13 September 2023 |date=6 January 2019}}</ref>

===Leeds Rhinos===
In October 2019, Gale signed a three-year contract with his hometown club [[Leeds Rhinos]] for an undisclosed fee.<ref>{{cite web |title=Luke Gale: Leeds Rhinos sign Castleford Tigers & England half-back |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/49883977 |website=BBC Sport |access-date=13 September 2023 |date=1 October 2019}}</ref> He captained Leeds in the [[2020 Challenge Cup]] final against [[Salford Red Devils]], with Gale scoring the winning drop-goal in the 76th minute of the game to edge Leeds to a 17–16 victory.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/54555957|title=Leeds beat Salford 17–16 after Luke Gale lands late winning drop-goal|work=BBC Sport }}</ref> In July 2021, Gale was stripped of the captaincy,<ref>{{cite web |title=Luke Gale: Leeds Rhinos stand down England half-back from captaincy |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/57972327 |website=BBC Sport |access-date=13 September 2023 |date=26 July 2021}}</ref> which he later revealed was due to a disagreement with Leeds coach [[Richard Agar]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Luke Gale lifts the lid on his Leeds Rhinos exit and captaincy incident |url=https://www.leeds-live.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/luke-gale-leeds-rhinos-exit-22118216 |website=Leeds Live |access-date=13 September 2023 |date=9 November 2021}}</ref>

===Hull F.C.===
On 5 November 2021 it was announced that he had signed for [[Hull F.C.]] in the [[Super League]].<ref>{{Cite web|date=5 November 2021|title=Luke Gale: Hull FC sign former Man of Steel from Leeds Rhinos|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/59185547|access-date=13 January 2022|website=BBC Sport}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|website=Love Rugby League|date=5 November 2021|accessdate=7 November 2021|title=Hull FC confirm Luke Gale signing|url=https://www.loverugbyleague.com/post/hull-fc-confirm-luke-gale-signing/}}</ref>

In round 1 of the 2022 Super League season, he made his club debut and scored a try in Hull F.C's 16–12 victory over [[Wakefield Trinity]].  The following week, he was sent off for a professional foul in the clubs 38–6 loss against [[St. Helens R.F.C.]]<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/news/12040/12546283/st-helens-boss-kristian-woolf-on-ugly-situation-as-luke-gale-sent-off-on-hull-fc-debut/|title=St Helens boss Kristian Woolf on 'ugly situation' as Luke Gale sent off on Hull FC debut|website=www.skysports.com}}</ref>
On 21 February, Gale was suspended for five matches over the red card against St Helens.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.hulldailymail.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/luke-gale-hull-fc-disciplinary-6704102.amp/|title=Luke Gale discovers length of suspension after disciplinary hearing concludes|website=www.hulldailymail.co.uk}}</ref>

===Keighley Cougars===
Gale became a [[free agent]] at the end of the 2022 season as his contract with Hull expired. On 19 December 2022 he signed a one-year contract to join Keighley in the Championship for 2023.<ref>{{cite news |title=Cougars come up trumps with super signing of former Leeds and Bradford star Gale |url=https://www.thetelegraphandargus.co.uk/sport/23201616.keighley-cougars-sign-former-bradford-bulls-leeds-rhinos-star/ |access-date=19 December 2022 |work=Bradford Telegraph and Argus |date=19 December 2022}}</ref>

Part-way through the 2023 season and after nine appearances for Keighley, Gale left the club to join Wakefield.

===Wakefield Trinity===
Gale signed a contract to keep him at Wakefield until the end of the 2024 season.<ref>{{cite web |title=Super League return confirmed for former Man of Steel Luke Gale |url=https://www.loverugbyleague.com/post/luke-gale-wakefield-super-league-return-keighley |website=LoveRugbyLeague |access-date=1 May 2023 |date=1 May 2023}}</ref>
Gale played ten matches for Wakefield Trinity in the [[Super League XXVIII]] season as the club finished bottom of the table and were relegated to the [[RFL Championship]] which ended their 24-year stay in the top flight.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/news/20876/12962152/super-league-wakefield-trinity-relegated-after-loss-at-leigh-leopards-st-helens-and-wigan-warriors-win/|title=Super League: Wakefield Trinity relegated after loss at Leigh Leopards, St Helens and Wigan Warriors win|website=www.skysports.com}}</ref>
In the 2024 RFL Championship season, Gale played 25 matches as the club won the 1895 Cup, league leaders shield and the RFL Championship grand final.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/articles/cqjr5lvxk4lo|title=Jowitt breaks points record as Wakefield cruise to Grand Final win|website=www.bbc.co.uk|date=19 October 2024 }}</ref>

==International career==
In 2011 and 2012, Gale played for the [[England Knights]] team. He kicked 8 goals, as well as scoring 1 try, in the Knights' 56–4 victory over Ireland in the [[2012 European Cup (rugby league)|2012 European Cup]].

After an outstanding 2015 Super League season, Gale was named in the senior [[England national rugby league team|England]] team for their end-of-year internationals against [[France national rugby league team|France]] and [[2015 New Zealand rugby league tour of Great Britain|New Zealand]],<ref>{{cite news 
|title = MCNAMARA NAMES 24-MAN SQUAD FOR 2015 INTERNATIONAL SERIES
|publisher = englandrl.co.uk
|date = 11 October 2015
|url = http://www.englandrl.co.uk/article/47305/mcnamara-names-24-man-squad-for
|access-date = 24 October 2015}}</ref> but did not make an appearance. He was selected again in England's squad for the [[2016 Four Nations]], making his international début in a [[2016 Rugby League Four Nations#France vs England|test match]] against France.

He was selected in the England squad for the [[2017 Rugby League World Cup]],<ref name="England rugby league World Cup squad revealed - and James Roby is back in the fold">{{cite web|url=https://www.mirror.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/james-roby-back-fold-wayne-11312505|title=England rugby league World Cup squad revealed – and James Roby is back in the fold|publisher=Mirror|date=9 October 2017|access-date=30 October 2019}}</ref> and subsequently played every game as England made the [[2017 Rugby League World Cup final|final]] in [[Melbourne]], where they lost narrowly to hosts [[Australia national rugby league team|Australia]].
